aliases: Malaysian Chicken Fried Rice with Sambal

english: Malaysian Chicken Fried Rice with Sambal

food_subgroup: Fried rice with poultry and chilli paste

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for nasi goreng with chicken and sambal; closest parent 'fried rice food product' (FOODON_00002694) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FHR (Fried rice) is the closest parent; no specific code exists for nasi goreng chicken sambal variant.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for nasi goreng or sambal fried rice; left null.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of nasi goreng chicken sambal as typically served at a Malaysian or Singaporean hawker stall or casual restaurant, approximately 350 g.

nutrient_source.title: Nasi Goreng (Fried Rice with Chicken) — IMR Malaysia MyFCD / HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition Data
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) and Institute for Medical Research Malaysia (IMR MyFCD)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists nasi goreng (chicken) as a composite dish. Values per 100 g are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo nasi goreng chicken entry and cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD fried rice with chicken data. Sambal component increases sodium and fat slightly; values adjusted to reflect sambal variant based on published HPB composite data for spicy fried rice variants.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — fried rice and rice-based mixed dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for nasi goreng chicken sambal. GI of 64 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for fried rice (white rice base, mixed dish), which reports GI values in the range 56–72 for Asian fried rice preparations. A midpoint value of 64 is used, consistent with medium-GI classification. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (64 × 78.75) / 100 ≈ 50.4, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 22.5 × 3.5 = 78.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from sambal paste, soy sauce, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A single 350 g serving provides approximately 1470 mg sodium, representing 64–98% of the recommended daily limit of 1500–2300 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and sambal or limit portion size to 150–200 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (64) and high glycemic load per serving (~50) from white rice base
type2_diabetes.guidance: The medium GI and high glycemic load per standard serving can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portions to 150–200 g, pair with additional non-starchy vegetables, and monitor blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat and high sodium from sambal and soy sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of moderate saturated fat (1.4 g per 100 g) and high sodium (420 mg per 100 g) warrants caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease; choosing smaller portions and requesting less oil and soy sauce can reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nasi Goreng Chicken Sambal is a Malaysian and Singaporean spicy fried rice dish made by stir-frying pre-cooked white rice with marinated chicken pieces, sambal chilli paste, egg, shallots, garlic, and soy sauce in a hot wok. The dish is characterised by its bold, smoky, and spicy flavour profile derived from the sambal — a cooked chilli paste typically made from dried red chillies, shrimp paste (belacan), shallots, and lime juice. The rice grains are coated in the aromatic sambal and rendered slightly caramelised from high-heat wok cooking. Texture is firm and slightly chewy with tender chicken pieces. It is commonly served with a fried egg on top, cucumber slices, and prawn crackers (keropok). Nutritionally, a standard 350 g plate provides approximately 612 kcal, 29.8 g protein, 21.7 g fat, and 78.8 g carbohydrate. The dish is energy-dense and high in sodium (approximately 1470 mg per serving), making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Its medium glycemic index (~64) and high glycemic load per serving (~50) require portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is halal-certified in most Malaysian and Singaporean establishments and is dairy-free. It is a staple at hawker centres, kopitiams, and casual restaurants across Malaysia and Singapore.
common_user_queries: What is nasi goreng chicken sambal?

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo or IMR MyFCD entry for nasi goreng chicken sambal as a distinct dish; values adapted from HPB Singapore nasi goreng chicken composite data and cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD fried rice with chicken. Sambal component adjustment is estimated. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database fried rice data. Confidence is medium rather than high due to these adaptations.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or IMR MyFCD source databases for this dish category. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the source databases for this dish. All other -1 values have been replaced with estimated or reported numeric values where available from approved sources. GI of 64 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database fried rice entries; no direct GI measurement for nasi goreng chicken sambal was found in the approved GI literature. Sodium value reflects sambal and soy sauce contribution and is a key clinical concern. This dish is typically halal in Malaysian and Singaporean contexts but consumers should verify with individual restaurants. The food_group 'Meat and alternatives' is preserved from the input record as the primary protein component is chicken.

improvement_actions_taken: Populated all five mandatory multilingual local_names for Malaysian/Singaporean cuisine (Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi).
Built comprehensive aliases list with 14 entries and verified consistency with aliases_by_language.
Sourced nutrient values from HPB Singapore Foodo and IMR Malaysia MyFCD as primary approved regional sources.
Documented GI as analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database with full estimation basis.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, selenium_mcg) in notes field.
Provided specific clinical guidance for all three required health conditions.
Ensured ontology match_type values use only allowed values: exact, closest_parent, none.
Verified no zero core macros requiring justification phrases.
